result:
I think of a river first. Not a gentle, babbling one, but a wide, slow-moving, powerful river the color of wet clay, cutting its patient course through a landscape of memory and loose ends. It carries things. Twigs, of course, and leaves. But also the glint of a lost earring, the faint smell of a Sunday roast from a house long demolished, a blurred photograph of people whose names are forgotten, the echo of a half-remembered argument, and the cool, smooth weight of a river stone worn perfectly oval by centuries of motion.

I have 1000 words. A finite space. A container. Like a single breath held, or a square of sunlight moving across a floor. It feels like both a freedom and a constraint. I could describe the precise way the light falls through my window right now—how it highlights the dust motes, turning them into tiny, frantic galaxies, and warms the edge of the coffee mug, a chip in its rim catching the light like a tiny beacon. I could tell you about the sound of distant traffic, a constant, low-grade hum that is the auditory signature of this era, the white noise of human movement.

But the river pulls me back. It feels truer. Life feels less like a narrative and more like that river—a collection, a confluence. Some days you are the bank, solid and defined. Other days you are the water, rushing forward without a thought. Most days you are something carried along in the current, that odd leaf that spins in a gentle, persistent eddy, going nowhere but always moving.

A memory surfaces, bobbing like an apple. I am seven, and my grandfather is teaching me to whittle. His hands are vast, mapped with blue veins and fine, white scars. He gives me a soft piece of pine and a blunt knife. “Always cut away from you,” he says, his voice a soft rumble. “The wood will tell you what it wants to be.” I hack at it nervously, producing not a boat or a whistle, but a sad, faceless shape. He takes it from me, turns it over in his palms. “Ah,” he says, his eyes crinkling. “A cloud. Very good.” In that moment, he didn’t see failure; he saw a cloud. He was a translator of intentions. He died when I was twelve, and I cannot remember the sound of his voice anymore, only the feeling of it, like stones rolling in a deep pocket.

That’s the thing about 1000 words. You can’t get it all in. You have to choose which fragments to save from the current. The smell of his pipe tobacco (cherry and oak). The way he pronounced “pecan” as “pee-can,” which made me giggle. The terrifying, thrilling way he’d throw me into the deep end of the lake, shouting, “Swim or sink!” You have to let most of it swirl past, unnamed, into the murk.

I think of all the unsent letters piling up in the back of the mind. The apologies, the declarations, the simple “I saw this and thought of you” notes that never found a stamp. They form a sediment on the riverbed, layers of compressed sentiment. Maybe writing is a kind of dredging. Pulling up a bucketful of that muck to see what’s intact.

Outside, a bird calls—a sharp, questioning note. It pulls me from the internal waterway to the solid world. The sun has moved. The square of light is now a parallelogram on the rug. The coffee is gone, just a bitter, cold trace at the bottom of the cup. This is the present. The solid chair, the slight ache in my lower back from sitting too long, the blinking cursor on the screen. It is so immediate, so *real*, and yet it feels thinner, somehow, than the memory of the cloud-whittling or the imagined river.

Perhaps that’s why we need stories. To give the present the weight of the past and the gravity of possibility. To weave the fleeting moment (this dust mote, this birdcall) into the larger, stronger tapestry of meaning. A single moment is a leaf. A story is the tree, the soil, and the season.

I am aware of the word count now, ticking down in the corner of my mind. It’s like watching a sand timer. The grains are finite. I want to use them to say something important, but what is more important than a river, or a grandfather’s kindness, or the shape of light in a quiet room?

Maybe the point is just to pay attention. To be the scribe for the ordinary miracle. The miracle of consciousness, here, now, in this body, on this planet spinning in the vast and silent dark. To notice the link between the chill of the river stone and the warmth of the sun on the rug. To draw a line, however faint, between them.

We are all building a raft from the debris we gather as we float. We lash memories to regrets, tie dreams to practical knots, use love as the cord that holds it all together. It’s never seaworthy, not really. It leaks. It lists. But it’s ours, and it keeps us from going under entirely.

The river doesn’t care. It just flows. Past the ruins of old castles and the scaffolding of new buildings. Past lovers on the bank and bodies weighted down in the deeps. It carries the glitter and the garbage with the same implacable grace.

My 1000 words are nearly spent. I’ll use the last few to say this: Look. Listen. Feel the current against your skin. Gather your fragments. Be kind. Whittle clouds. And when you get a chance, in the quiet between the traffic noise and the birdcall, send the letter.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
